content.text: Rollback Strategies for Prompt Versions

Prompt regressions in production can manifest as wrong output categories, increased refusals, malformed JSON, higher latency, or elevated cost. Because prompts are versioned as immutable artifacts, restoring previous behavior is a configuration change — no re-deployment of code and no rebuilding of binaries.
Rollback must be a one-command operation that any on-call engineer can execute. If it requires more than that, it will be delayed exactly when speed matters most.
This sub-subtopic covers the three rollback modes, how to detect when each is appropriate, schema compatibility risks, and the post-mortem process that turns an incident into a durable improvement.

attrs.alt: Decision tree for prompt rollback: immediate config rollback for recent version changes, schema-compatibility check before non-emergency rollbacks

content.text: Three Rollback Modes
Mode
When to use
Time to resolution
Risk
Immediate config rollback
Error rate spike within minutes of version change
< 5 minutes
Low — no code changes
Scheduled config rollback
Regression detected after hours; canary shows clear trend
15–30 minutes with review
Low if schemas match
Schema-aware rollback
New version changed output schema; consumers already adapted
Hours — needs shim or consumer update
Medium — requires coordination
The first question on any incident is whether a prompt version changed in the recent deployment window. The version endpoint makes this check trivial.
Immediate Config Rollback Procedure
For a prompt version deployed in the last hour that is clearly the cause of the incident:

#!/bin/bash
# On-call runbook: rollback prompt to previous version
# Usage: ./rollback_prompt.sh <prompt-key> <rollback-to-version>

set -euo pipefail
PROMPT_KEY="$1"
ROLLBACK_VERSION="$2"
ENV="production"
CONFIG="deploy/config/${ENV}.yaml"
REGISTRY_BUCKET="acme-prompt-registry"
PROMPT_NAME="triage-v2"   # derive from PROMPT_KEY in real script

echo "=== ROLLBACK: $PROMPT_KEY to $ROLLBACK_VERSION in $ENV ==="

# 1. Fetch the SHA-256 for the rollback target from the registry
SHA=$(aws s3 cp \
  s3://${REGISTRY_BUCKET}/prompts/${PROMPT_NAME}/${ROLLBACK_VERSION}/${PROMPT_NAME}-${ROLLBACK_VERSION}.sha256 \
  - | awk '{print $1}')
echo "SHA-256: $SHA"

# 2. Update the production config
python3 - <<PYEOF
import yaml
cfg = yaml.safe_load(open("$CONFIG"))
cfg["prompts"]["$PROMPT_KEY"]["version"] = "$ROLLBACK_VERSION"
cfg["prompts"]["$PROMPT_KEY"]["sha256"] = "$SHA"
open("$CONFIG", "w").write(yaml.dump(cfg, default_flow_style=False))
print("Config updated.")
PYEOF

# 3. Commit with rollback annotation
git add "$CONFIG"
git commit -m "hotfix($PROMPT_KEY): rollback to $ROLLBACK_VERSION (incident response)"
git push origin main

echo "=== Rollback committed. CD pipeline will deploy within 2 minutes. ==="
echo "Monitor: https://grafana.example.com/d/prompt-ops?var-env=$ENV"
The CD pipeline detects the push, pulls the pinned artifact from the registry, verifies its hash, and deploys — the same path as any other promotion.
Verifying the Rollback
After the CD pipeline completes, verify that the intended version is actually serving traffic:

# scripts/verify_rollback.py
import requests, time, sys

VERSION_ENDPOINT = "https://api.example.com/internal/prompt-versions"
EXPECTED_VERSION = sys.argv[1]       # e.g. "1.2.0"
PROMPT_KEY = sys.argv[2]             # e.g. "triage"
TIMEOUT_SECS = 300

deadline = time.time() + TIMEOUT_SECS
while time.time() < deadline:
    try:
        resp = requests.get(VERSION_ENDPOINT, timeout=5)
        versions = resp.json()
        current = versions.get(PROMPT_KEY, {}).get("version")
        if current == EXPECTED_VERSION:
            print(f"VERIFIED: {PROMPT_KEY} is now serving {EXPECTED_VERSION}")
            sys.exit(0)
        print(f"Waiting... current={current}, want={EXPECTED_VERSION}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Check failed: {e}")
    time.sleep(10)

print(f"TIMEOUT: version did not converge to {EXPECTED_VERSION} within {TIMEOUT_SECS}s")
sys.exit(1)

Run this script from the on-call terminal immediately after the push. It polls the live version endpoint rather than relying on deploy logs, so it confirms actual request routing rather than deployment success.
Schema Compatibility and Rollback Risk

A MAJOR version bump signals a breaking change in the output schema. Rolling back across a MAJOR version boundary is risky when downstream consumers have already been updated to handle the new schema.

# scripts/check_schema_compat.py
import json, semver, sys
from pathlib import Path

def load_schema(prompt_name: str, version: str) -> dict:
    path = Path(f"prompts/{prompt_name}/{version}/schema.json")
    return json.loads(path.read_text())

def check_compat(prompt_name: str, from_version: str, to_version: str) -> bool:
    from_v = semver.Version.parse(from_version)
    to_v   = semver.Version.parse(to_version)

    if to_v.major < from_v.major:
        print(f"WARNING: rollback crosses MAJOR boundary "
              f"({from_version} -> {to_version})")
        schema_from = load_schema(prompt_name, from_version)
        schema_to   = load_schema(prompt_name, to_version)
        # Check that every field in the current schema exists in the rollback schema
        from_fields = set(schema_from.get("properties", {}).keys())
        to_fields   = set(schema_to.get("properties", {}).keys())
        dropped = from_fields - to_fields
        if dropped:
            print(f"INCOMPATIBLE: fields present in {from_version} missing in "
                  f"{to_version}: {dropped}")
            return False
        print("Schema fields are compatible (rollback target is a strict subset).")

    return True

if not check_compat(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]):
    sys.exit(1)
Schema Shim for Incompatible Rollbacks

When a rollback is necessary but schemas are incompatible, a thin shim in the response-processing layer translates the old output format to what the current consumer expects. This is a temporary bridge — it ships at the same time as the rollback and is removed once the root-cause fix is deployed.
The shim uses the prompt_version field logged with every call to decide which parsing path to take. Remove the shim in the next release once the root-cause prompt fix has soaked in canary.
Automating Rollback from Alerting
For time-critical regressions, the monitoring system can trigger a rollback automatically. The alert calls a webhook that opens a GitHub Actions workflow run with the rollback parameters:

# .github/workflows/prompt-rollback.yml
name: Emergency Prompt Rollback

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      prompt_key:
        description: "Prompt to roll back (e.g. triage)"
        required: true
      rollback_version:
        description: "Target version (e.g. 1.2.0)"
        required: true
      reason:
        description: "Incident ticket or description"
        required: true

jobs:
  rollback:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production-emergency    # requires 1 approver
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Fetch SHA-256 for rollback version
        run: |
          PROMPT_NAME=$(python3 scripts/key_to_name.py ${{ inputs.prompt_key }})
          SHA=$(aws s3 cp \
            s3://acme-prompt-registry/prompts/$PROMPT_NAME/${{ inputs.rollback_version }}/$PROMPT_NAME-${{ inputs.rollback_version }}.sha256 \
            - | awk '{print $1}')
          echo "PROMPT_NAME=$PROMPT_NAME" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "SHA=$SHA" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Update production config
        run: |
          python3 scripts/set_prompt_version.py \
            --env production \
            --key ${{ inputs.prompt_key }} \
            --version ${{ inputs.rollback_version }} \
            --sha256 $SHA

      - name: Commit rollback
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git add deploy/config/production.yaml
          git commit -m "hotfix(${{ inputs.prompt_key }}): rollback to ${{ inputs.rollback_version }} — ${{ inputs.reason }}"
          git push origin main

      - name: Verify deployment
        run: |
          python3 scripts/verify_rollback.py \
            ${{ inputs.rollback_version }} \
            ${{ inputs.prompt_key }}
The
The
production-emergency

GitHub environment requires at least one reviewer to approve the workflow run before it executes. This adds a human check even for automated rollbacks, preventing alert storms from rolling back a healthy system.
Rollback Log and Post-Mortem
Every rollback — automatic or manual — must produce a structured log entry that feeds the post-mortem process. The log is append-only and stored in Git:

# docs/rollbacks/2026-07-01-triage-v1.3.0.yaml
date: "2026-07-01T16:42:00Z"
prompt: triage-v2
rolled_back_from: "1.3.0"
rolled_back_to: "1.2.0"
trigger: automated   # or "manual"
incident: INC-2847
impact:
  duration_minutes: 7
  error_rate_peak: 0.04
  estimated_affected_requests: 1200
root_cause: |
  The new system prompt in 1.3.0 introduced the phrase "respond only with JSON"
  which conflicted with the model's safety layer on edge-case inputs, causing
  empty responses on ~4% of requests.
fix:
  description: Removed the imperative phrase; added a few-shot example instead.
  pr: 448
  version_fixed_in: "1.3.1"
schema_compat: true
shim_required: false
action_items:
  - Add empty-response rate to the staging eval gate (PR #449)
  - Update prompt authoring guide: avoid imperative-only JSON instructions

File one YAML per rollback in a versioned directory. Review them in the next sprint retrospective to identify patterns — recurring rollbacks on a particular prompt signal a weak eval suite, not a bad author.
Preventing Future Rollbacks
The most effective rollback strategy is one you never need to execute. After each rollback, add the failure case to the eval suite before the next version is authored:
Add the incident case to the full eval suite with a note referencing the incident ticket.
Set the eval gate threshold to fail on empty or unparseable responses (not just on category mismatch).
Re-run the staging eval on the previous offending version to confirm the new case would have caught it.
Summary
Rollback is a config change:
re-pin the previous version + SHA-256 in the deployment config and push — no code change, no re-build.
Three modes:
immediate (< 5 min, clear cause), scheduled (with review), schema-aware (needs shim when schemas diverge across MAJOR versions).
Version endpoint:
expose a health route that reports loaded prompt name, version, and hash; use it to confirm rollback completion.
Schema compatibility check:
run before any rollback that crosses a MAJOR version boundary to detect consumer breakage.
Schema shim:
a thin translation layer deployed alongside the rollback when schemas are incompatible; remove it with the root-cause fix.
Automated rollback workflow:
GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch with a production-emergency environment gate; triggered by alert webhook.
Rollback log:
append a structured YAML entry for every rollback; review in retrospectives to detect weak eval coverage.
Close the loop:
add the failing case to the eval suite before the next version ships; a rollback that does not update evals will repeat.
